prompt: Translate the following English sentence into French, Japanese, and Arabic, and add a brief note on any phrase that was tricky to render faithfully: "The silence between us said more than any argument could have."
response:
French  
« Le silence entre nous en disait plus que n’importe quelle dispute. »  
Note: “said more than any argument” becomes “en disait plus que n’importe quelle dispute”; the verb “en dire” keeps the idea of “speaking/communicating,” while “dispute” is the closest natural equivalent for “argument” in the sense of a verbal fight.

Japanese  
私たちの間の沈黙が、どんな口論よりも多くを語っていた。  
Note: 口論 (kōron) is a common noun for “verbal argument,” but the real work is done by 多くを語っていた (ōku o katatte ita), literally “was saying much,” to convey that the silence communicated more than any words.

Arabic  
السكوت بين قولَيْنا أكثرُ تعبيرًا من أيةِ جدال.  
Note: السكوت بين قولَيْنا literally means “the silence between our two sayings,” a compact way to express “between us.” جدال is the standard word for “argument/disputation,” but the tricky part was keeping the comparative “more expressive than any argument” within Arabic’s compact phrasing without sounding stilted.
